  "opinion": "I think there's definitely a problem with women not being represented enough in science, and I believe we should do something about it. Having been around for 55 years as an American woman living in New York City, I've seen firsthand how certain fields are dominated by men, even outside of work like at the grocery store where they might be handling all the electronics or asking questions about new gadgets.

As someone who owns her own hairdressing business and has a husband with higher education involved, I know that sometimes people think science requires formal schooling which isn't my background. But honestly, it's more about passion than papers for me anyway—just like when you're cutting hair or styling a special event look; some of the best ideas come from everyday observations.

I'm married and have two kids who go to school here in New York where we live. My mother-in-law actually has that bachelor's degree thing, so maybe I should ask her about what she thinks—though being an atheist myself without much formal schooling beyond my early life experiences dealing with numbers for business purposes.

I volunteer at local events too because agreeableness is part of who I am; helping out makes sense when you see things like this. And looking back on all those times working with clients and family members through tough situations, good mental health has helped me manage stress well.

So yes, addressing the gender gap seems necessary even if we don't rely solely on traditional education paths or just STEM degrees.",

response_parsed.topic: What is the gender gap in science? Should we address it? And how?
response_parsed.opinion:
I think there's definitely a problem with women not being represented enough in science, and I believe we should do something about it. Having been around for 55 years as an American woman living in New York City, I've seen firsthand how certain fields are dominated by men, even outside of work like at the grocery store where they might be handling all the electronics or asking questions about new gadgets.

As someone who owns her own hairdressing business and has a husband with higher education involved, I know that sometimes people think science requires formal schooling which isn't my background. But honestly, it's more about passion than papers for me anyway—just like when you're cutting hair or styling a special event look; some of the best ideas come from everyday observations.

I'm married and have two kids who go to school here in New York where we live. My mother-in-law actually has that bachelor's degree thing, so maybe I should ask her about what she thinks—though being an atheist myself without much formal schooling beyond my early life experiences dealing with numbers for business purposes.

I volunteer at local events too because agreeableness is part of who I am; helping out makes sense when you see things like this. And looking back on all those times working with clients and family members through tough situations, good mental health has helped me manage stress well.

So yes, addressing the gender gap seems necessary even if we don't rely solely on traditional education paths or just STEM degrees.
